edges.description: Nucleic-acid-containing immune complexes can be routed to plasmacytoid dendritic cells, where endosomal sensing produces IFN-alpha and amplifies the systemic interferon loop.
intermediate_mechanisms: Fc receptor and complement-dependent immune-complex handling
edges.target: Nucleic Acid Immune Complex-pDC Endosomal Loop

edges.description: Nucleic-acid immune complexes can co-engage Fc receptors and endosomal TLR7/TLR9, activating B cells, plasmacytoid dendritic cells, and kidney-resident inflammatory pathways.
intermediate_mechanisms: Fc receptor-mediated uptake of nucleic-acid immune complexes
delivery of RNA and DNA ligands to endosomal TLR7 and TLR9

edges.description: Immune complexes activate complement locally while inherited or acquired defects in early complement handling impair clearance of immune complexes and apoptotic debris.
Some nucleic-acid immune complexes localize in kidney glomeruli, making renal tissue one major downstream context for immune-complex injury.
Autoantibody immune complexes can contribute to skin injury, providing a tissue-specific branch parallel to nephritis.
Joint inflammation is modeled as another immune-complex deposition context, while the exact tissue determinants remain less resolved than for lupus nephritis.
Immune complex deposition in glomeruli triggers local inflammation and tissue damage.
intermediate_mechanisms: Fc receptor and Toll-like receptor amplification
renal cytokine and chemokine recruitment of inflammatory cells

edges.description: Type I interferon is modeled as a systemic amplifier of autoreactive B-cell and autoantibody circuits, while the exact perpetuation mechanisms remain an open knowledge gap.
High type I interferon identifies a subgroup with more active disease and greater nephritis propensity.
Nucleic-acid immune-complex sensing by plasmacytoid dendritic cells is modeled as a proximate ignition route for IFN-alpha/type I interferon production.
In renal tissue, immune-complex Fc receptor and TLR co-signaling amplifies local inflammation after glomerular immune-complex formation.
pDC engagement by nucleic-acid immune complexes produces IFN-alpha, feeding the systemic type I interferon signature.
edges.source: Nucleic Acid Immune Complex-pDC Endosomal Loop

edges.description: Cytosolic self-DNA sensing can produce type I interferon and therefore acts as a non-endosomal ignition route for the interferon signature.
intermediate_mechanisms: cGAS activation or STING-dependent DNA sensing

edges.description: NET material provides nucleic-acid antigen and can directly activate plasmacytoid dendritic cells, promoting type I interferon production.
intermediate_mechanisms: NET-derived DNA, histones, and neutrophil proteins
edges.source: NETosis and Neutrophil Extracellular Trap Formation

edges.description: Oxidized mitochondrial DNA released during lupus-relevant NET biology can feed cytosolic DNA sensing and STING-dependent interferon signaling.
edges.source: NETosis and Neutrophil Extracellular Trap Formation

edges.description: Impaired NET dismantling increases persistent DNA/histone/neutrophil-protein antigenic material and is associated with renal involvement.
edges.source: NETosis and Neutrophil Extracellular Trap Formation

edges.description: Expanded autoreactive B-cell and plasma-cell programs produce antinuclear and anti-dsDNA antibodies that feed immune-complex formation.
intermediate_mechanisms: anti-dsDNA and other antinuclear antibody production

edges.description: Defective apoptotic-cell clearance increases the self-antigen substrate available for autoreactive antibody binding and immune-complex formation.
Defective C1q-mediated immune-complex handling can redirect immune complexes toward plasmacytoid dendritic cells and IFN-alpha production.
Complement activation downstream of immune-complex deposition amplifies local inflammatory injury, particularly in lupus nephritis.
Dysregulated T cell programs promote autoreactive B cell help and autoantibody production
edges.source: Epigenetic Dysregulation and T Cell Metabolic Reprogramming

nodes.description: Age-associated B cells (ABCs) are enriched in SLE, characterized by T-bet and CD11c expression. They correlate with IFN signatures and disease activity, linking IFN-BAFF-IL-21 circuits to persistent autoantibody production.

Early component complement deficiencies (C1q, C3, C4) and complement consumption occur alongside immune complex deposition. Classical pathway activation by immune complexes contributes to tissue injury, particularly in lupus nephritis.

evidence.reference_title: Complement, interferon and lupus.
evidence.snippet: promoting the clearance of immune complexes (ICs) and apoptotic cells.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanisms of tissue injury in lupus nephritis.
evidence.snippet: Deposition of complement proteins in glomeruli is a key feature of lupus nephritis.

nodes.description: Immune complexes deposit at the dermal-epidermal junction and in vessel walls of the skin, leading to cutaneous manifestations of lupus.

evidence.reference_title: Autoantibodies in systemic lupus erythematosus: From immunopathology to therapeutic target.
evidence.snippet: The autoantibodies, especially anti-dsDNA and anti-Sm autoantibodies are highly specific to SLE, and participate in the immune complex formation and inflammatory damage on multiple end-organs such as kidney, skin, and central nervous system (CNS).

evidence.reference_title: The pathogenesis of systemic lupus erythematosus.
evidence.snippet: Systemic lupus erythematosus is an autoimmune disease that causes inflammation in the tissues of the brain, endothelial cells, gastrointestinal/genitourinary (GI/GU), joints, kidneys, muscles, and skin. Lupus comprises a range of multisystem disorders involving the deposition of aberrant immune complexes into tissues.

nodes.description: Cytosolic DNA sensing provides a parallel route to type I interferon activation outside the endosomal TLR7/TLR9 loop. In SLE, interferogenic mitochondrial DNA from NET biology and mitochondria-retaining erythroid cells can engage STING- or cGAS-dependent pathways in myeloid cells.

evidence.reference_title: Neutrophil extracellular traps enriched in oxidized mitochondrial DNA are interferogenic and contribute to lupus-like disease.
evidence.snippet: Extracellular release of oxidized mitochondrial DNA is proinflammatory in vitro, and when this DNA is injected into mice, it stimulates type I interferon (IFN) signaling through a pathway dependent on the DNA sensor STING.

evidence.reference_title: Erythroid mitochondrial retention triggers myeloid-dependent type I interferon in human SLE.
evidence.snippet: SLE patients carrying both Mito+ RBCs and opsonizing antibodies display the highest levels of blood IFN-stimulated gene (ISG) signatures, a distinctive feature of SLE.

nodes.description: Aberrant DNA methylation, histone modifications, and metabolic reprogramming in T cells contribute to lupus pathogenesis. Hypomethylation of immune gene loci activates autoreactive T cells, while metabolic shifts including altered mTOR signaling and glutaminolysis support inflammatory T cell programs.
nodes.id: Epigenetic Dysregulation and T Cell Metabolic Reprogramming

nodes.description: Antinuclear, anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, and related autoantibodies bind nucleic acids, nucleosomes, and nucleic-acid-binding proteins to form immune complexes that can deposit in tissues or induce cytokine production.

evidence.reference_title: New insights into the role of antinuclear antibodies in systemic lupus erythematosus.
evidence.snippet: antinuclear antibodies (ANAs) that form immune complexes

evidence.reference_title: Autoantibodies in systemic lupus erythematosus: From immunopathology to therapeutic target.
evidence.snippet: participate in the immune complex formation and inflammatory damage on multiple end-organs such as kidney, skin, and central nervous system (CNS).

evidence.reference_title: Immune Cell Metabolism in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus.
evidence.snippet: Here, we review what is known on the altered metabolic patterns of CD4(+) T cells, B cells, and myeloid cells in lupus patients and lupus-prone mice and how they contribute to lupus pathogenesis.

evidence.reference_title: TLR4(+)CXCR4(+) plasma cells drive nephritis development in systemic lupus erythematosus.
evidence.snippet: TLR4(+)CXCR4(+) plasma cells drive nephritis development in systemic lupus erythematosus.

evidence.reference_title: Immune checkpoints and the multiple faces of B cells in systemic lupus erythematosus.
evidence.snippet: B-lymphocytes are crucial in the pathogenesis of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), including autoantibody production, antigen presentation, co-stimulation, and cytokine secretion.

evidence.reference_title: Complement, interferon and lupus.
evidence.snippet: C1q opsonized apoptotic cells also exert an immunosuppressive effect through cytokine regulation and the stimulation of additional opsonins by macrophages.

nodes.description: Circulating immune complexes containing anti-dsDNA and anti-Sm antibodies lodge in kidney glomeruli, activating complement and recruiting inflammatory cells.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanisms of tissue injury in lupus nephritis.
evidence.snippet: Systemic lupus erythematosus is a prototypic autoimmune disease characterized by autoantibody production and immune complex formation/deposition in target organs such as the kidney.

evidence.reference_title: Deposition of Immune Complexes in Gingival Tissues in the Presence of Periodontitis and Systemic Lupus Erythematosus.
evidence.snippet: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a complex chronic autoimmune disease characterized by tissue damage and widespread inflammation in response to environmental challenges. Deposition of immune complexes in kidneys glomeruli are associated with lupus nephritis, determining SLE diagnosis.

evidence.reference_title: Antibodies targeting circulating protective molecules in lupus nephritis: Interest as serological biomarkers.
evidence.snippet: LN is characterized by glomerular kidney injury, essentially due to deposition of immune complexes involving autoantibodies against cellular components and circulating proteins.

nodes.description: Defective clearance of apoptotic cells and impaired efferocytosis lead to increased exposure to nuclear antigens, propagating immune complex formation and autoantibody production.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanisms of tissue injury in lupus nephritis.
evidence.snippet: Systemic lupus erythematosus is a prototypic autoimmune disease characterized by autoantibody production and immune complex formation/deposition in target organs such as the kidney. Resultant local inflammation then leads to organ damage.

evidence.reference_title: Unraveling the Link between Interferon-α and Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: From the Molecular Mechanisms to Target Therapies.
evidence.snippet: The clinical heterogeneity of the disease is accompanied by complex disturbances affecting the immune system with inflammation and tissue damage due to loss of tolerance to nuclear antigens and the deposition of immune complexes in tissues.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenesis of Human Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: A Cellular Perspective.
evidence.snippet: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a chronic autoimmune disease affecting multiple organs. A complex interaction of genetics, environment, and hormones leads to immune dysregulation and breakdown of tolerance to self-antigens, resulting in autoantibody production, inflammation, and destruction of end-organs.

evidence.reference_title: Deposition of Immune Complexes in Gingival Tissues in the Presence of Periodontitis and Systemic Lupus Erythematosus.
evidence.snippet: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a complex chronic autoimmune disease characterized by tissue damage and widespread inflammation in response to environmental challenges.

evidence.reference_title: Biomarkers for systemic lupus erythematosus - a focus on organ damage.
evidence.snippet: In the current review, we focus on the commonly affected organs (skin, kidney, and nervous system) in SLE to summarize the emerging biomarkers that show promise in disease diagnosis, monitoring and treatment response assessment.

evidence.reference_title: Systemic lupus erythematosus (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0033726 | Lupus nephritis | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Morbidity and mortality in systemic lupus erythematosus during a 10-year period: a comparison of early and late manifestations in a cohort of 1,000 patients.
evidence.snippet: 279 (27.9%) active nephropathy

evidence.reference_title: Lupus nephritis.
evidence.snippet: Lupus nephritis is one of the more serious manifestations of the systemic autoimmune disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, and is associated with considerable morbidity and even mortality.

evidence.reference_title: Risk of Renal Failure Within 10 or 20 Years of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Diagnosis.
evidence.snippet: The frequency of endstage renal disease (ESRD) from systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in the United States has not improved over the last few decades in large population datasets.

evidence.reference_title: Lymphocytes in the neighborhood: good or bad for the kidney?
evidence.snippet: Lupus nephritis (LN) is common in people with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) and advances, almost invariably, to end-stage renal disease (ESRD).

evidence.reference_title: [Renal involvement in systemic lupus erythematosus].
evidence.snippet: Renal involvement is frequent (20 to 50% of cases) during the course of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).

nodes.description: Neutrophils undergo enhanced NETosis, releasing extracellular traps (NETs) composed of DNA, histones, and granule proteins. Impaired NET degradation due to deficient serum DNase1 amplifies the pDC-TLR9-IFN-alpha loop and provides modified autoantigens. NETs are associated with lupus nephritis.
nodes.id: NETosis and Neutrophil Extracellular Trap Formation

evidence.reference_title: Impairment of neutrophil extracellular trap degradation is associated with lupus nephritis.
evidence.snippet: Neutrophils respond to infections and release extracellular traps (NETs), which are antimicrobial and are made of DNA, histones, and neutrophil proteins.

nodes.description: Nucleic-acid-containing immune complexes are routed to plasmacytoid dendritic cells and endosomal nucleic-acid sensors, generating IFN-alpha and reinforcing the autoantibody, immune-complex, and interferon circuit. Complement handling, especially C1q-dependent clearance, modulates whether immune complexes are cleared or preferentially engage pDCs.
nodes.id: Nucleic Acid Immune Complex-pDC Endosomal Loop

evidence.reference_title: Complement, interferon and lupus.
evidence.snippet: in the absence of C1q, instead of ICs binding to monocytes, they preferentially engage plasmacytoid dendritic cells (pDC) so generating interferon (IFN) alpha

nodes.description: Immune complexes accumulate in synovial fluid and tissue of joints, activating complement and triggering inflammatory arthritis.

evidence.reference_title: The pathogenesis of systemic lupus erythematosus.
evidence.snippet: Systemic lupus erythematosus is an autoimmune disease that causes inflammation in the tissues of the brain, endothelial cells, gastrointestinal/genitourinary (GI/GU), joints, kidneys, muscles, and skin. Lupus comprises a range of multisystem disorders involving the deposition of aberrant immune complexes into tissues.

evidence.reference_title: Cardiovascular manifestations of systemic lupus erythematosus: current perspective.
evidence.snippet: Evidence is now slowly accumulating that substantiates that immune complex deposition, complement activation and subsequent inflammatory reaction is responsible for the majority of the cardiovascular manifestations of SLE, for example, pericarditis, myocarditis, endocarditis, coronary arteritis, coronary atherosclerosis, and systemic and pulmonary vasculitis.

evidence.reference_title: Bone Disease in Connective Tissue Disease/Systemic Lupus Erythematosus.
evidence.snippet: Recent studies demonstrated an increased incidence of osteoporosis and peripheral and vertebral fractures in patients with SLE

evidence.reference_title: Immune complex injury of the lung.
evidence.snippet: Abundant evidence currently exists to suggest that immune complexes play an important role in inflammatory diseases of the lung. ... systemic lupus erythematosus have been shown to be associated with the presence of immune complexes both in lung and in the serum.

nodes.description: B cell-intrinsic TLR7 signaling drives severe lupus through recognition of RNA-associated autoantigens, while TLR9 can exert counter-regulatory effects. TLR7/9 activation in plasmacytoid dendritic cells triggers type I interferon production.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanisms of tissue injury in lupus nephritis.
evidence.snippet: This type of two-step activation is known to activate B cells by ICs containing either TLR9 or TLR7 activators such as dsDNA or single-stranded RNA

nodes.description: Chronic activation of the type I interferon signaling pathway is a central pathophysiological mechanism in SLE, shaping dysregulation across immune cell lineages and correlating with disease activity and therapy response.

evidence.reference_title: Type I interferon in the pathogenesis of systemic lupus erythematosus.
evidence.snippet: Type I interferon (IFN) is a primary pathogenic factor in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).

evidence.reference_title: Type I interferon signature in systemic lupus erythematosus.
evidence.snippet: An increased expression of type I IFN-regulated genes, termed IFN signature, has been reported in patients with SLE.
